sop.coo_evaluation:
# Gaps — no SOP yet

- **Member complaint & incident escalation**: no documented process for handling complaints (class experience, billing, harassment, injury claims), dispute resolution timelines, or incident logging for H&S/liability
- **Staff disciplinary & performance management**: no SOP for underperformance conversations, capability plans, or termination procedures
- **Financial controls & audit**: no documented process for cash handling, petty cash reconciliation, invoice approval, expense sign-off, or monthly P&L review
- **Member health screening & medical exemptions**: no intake screening SOP or process for members with injuries/conditions requesting modifications
- **Coach absence & coverage**: no SOP for last-minute sick leave, cover assignment, or member notification when a coach can't deliver a booked class
- **Safeguarding & vulnerable members**: no SOP for identifying or supporting members in distress, or responding to safeguarding concerns
- **Retention win-back campaign**: no documented playbook for re-engaging lapsed members or handling churn reasons
- **Supplier & vendor management**: no SOP for onboarding, payment terms, performance review, or replacement of key vendors (cleaning, maintenance, IT)

---

# Needs updating

- **Client onboarding**: likely missing payment method setup, data protection consent, emergency contact confirmation, and first-class experience handover
- **Coaches daily SOP**: verify it covers class setup, member modifications, equipment checks, and post-class member feedback (not just coaching cues)
- **Marketing (10/12)**: your newsletter & blog SOPs aren't built—but before building those, audit whether email segmentation, content approval workflows, and performance tracking are actually in the existing 10
- **Facilities (4/4)**: probably missing deep-clean schedules, equipment maintenance logs, HVAC/climate control checks, and emergency procedures (fire, medical, facility damage)

kpi.advice:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO ADVICE

## INCREASE REVENUE

- **Pricing is leaving money on the table.** AR/M has collapsed from £170.43 (Dec) to £120.48 (Jun) — a 29% drop. You've grown headcount (147 members in Jun vs 124 in Dec) but revenue per member has tanked. Audit membership tiers immediately: introduce a premium tier (personal training add-ons, peak-hours-only cheaper tier to segment). Even a £5/month uplift across 147 members = £735/month net.
- **Churn spike in April (8.33%) and March (6.60%) is now stabilising (0% in Jun), but you're not converting the bigger space into new revenue.** With 47 extra square feet, target 160+ members by August through partner referrals (physiotherapists, nutritionists in Belfast) and a "refer a friend" scheme (£20 credit). At current AR/M of £120.48, each new member = £120+ baseline.
- **Non-class revenue is invisible.** Launch a small shop (branded water bottles, foam rollers, resistance bands — 40% margin) or nutrition plans (partner with a local coach, 20% commission). Even £500/month would lift June's £17.7k revenue by 2.8%.

## REDUCE EXPENSES

- **Non-people costs in Jan–Apr were catastrophic (£8.2k–£30.5k), April was relocation, but Jan–Mar averaged £11.2k/month vs Sept–Nov average of £4.6k.** Post-relocation (May–Jun), they've stabilised at £9.3k–£9.4k. That's still 2x pre-move. **Audit utilities, rent, insurance, software subscriptions immediately** — are you paying for old studio lease alongside new one? Can you negotiate utilities (bulk LED, smart thermostat)? You should target £6.5k/month non-people spend by August.
- **Payroll has crept to £3.7k in June (up from £2.5k in Nov).** You've added headcount for the bigger studio, understood — but at £17.7k revenue, a £3.7k payroll is 21% of turnover. For profitability, cap at 18% (£3.2k). If you've hired an extra coach or admin, evaluate utilisation: are all hours billable/scheduled? Shift non-billable tasks to part-time or outsource admin (Xero bookkeeper, ~£150/

staff.advice:
• **Redistribute Grace's 43 class sessions:** JoJo takes Build: Upper and Mobility (her strengths); Eilis takes Build: Lower, Elevate, Strongevity, and No Sweat Intro (low-risk, suitable for her current level). This spreads load and develops Eilis's range.

• **Move Grace's 9 PT scans to any coach:** Treat InBody scans as admin tasks, not coaching time. Assign them to whoever has capacity that week—this frees real PT slots for Eilis and JoJo.

• **Rebalance JoJo's PT load (currently 44 sessions):** She's carrying the majority. Grow Eilis to 15–18 PT slots per month by pairing her with lower-complexity clients or co-coaching initially. JoJo drops to ~30 PT, reducing burnout risk.

• **Plug Eilis into Build: Full Body and Plate classes:** She's only running 2 classes per month; these are her sweet spot. Add 2–3 recurring slots to build consistency and member confidence in her coaching.

• **Audit Tuesday coverage:** JoJo is anchoring Tuesdays alone. Confirm whether Eilis could co-lead one Tuesday Build session (non-technical warm-up or cool-down support) to start building her confidence for future solo Tuesday classes.

• **Monitor PT booking and feedback:** Once Grace steps away, track which coaches members book. If Eilis's bookings lag, consider a 1-month "try with Eilis" offer or pairing strategy to build her client base without overwhelming her.

class_stats.suggestion:
**For quiet studio time gaps:**

1. Launch a paid "Open Gym + Coach" slot on Saturdays (your quietest day) — 60 minutes where members can train freely with a coach available for form checks or quick programming questions, positioned as lighter-touch than PT but more structured than open access.

2. Add a 30-minute "Strength Basics Workshop" on Thursday mornings (quietest weekday) — rotating topics like deadlift setup, squat depth, or bar positioning — to drive Thursday attendance and feed members into the strength classes they're underrepresenting (Engine, Capacity).

**To lift the quiet classes themselves:**

3. Move **Engine** to a Tuesday or Wednesday slot (your busy days) rather than its current time, and run a 2-week "Engine Challenge" with a leaderboard to build momentum — Engine's low numbers suggest timing or visibility, not the class itself.

kpis.Action: INTERNAL: Create clear service menu (for coaches and for members) — what we offer, what it costs, what's included.
kpis.advice:
# Service Menu Build — Tactical Execution for Grace

**1. Audit what you're actually selling RIGHT NOW**
Before designing menus, spend 2 hours documenting every service The Forge delivers today: class types, PT packages, nutrition add-ons, challenges, guest passes, anything members pay for. Talk to your top 3 coaches — they'll reveal services you've forgotten you offer. This prevents designing a menu that contradicts reality.

**2. Price from retention/margin data, not guessing**
Pull your last 3 months of transactions. What sells most? What has the best margin? Build menus around *that*, not what sounds good. If one-to-one PT is driving 40% of revenue, that gets prominent positioning. If a package isn't selling, axe it from the menu rather than feature it.

**3. Create TWO separate documents with intentional design**
- **Coach menu**: Lists every service with exact delivery specs (e.g., "Intro PT = 30 min assessment + 45 min session"). This stops coaches improvising and undercharging.
- **Member menu**: Clean pricing tiers with *what's included* spelled out (classes unlimited? nutrition coaching extra?). Use tiered pricing ($29/$49/$79/month) — converts better than single options.

**4. Test-drive with 5 members THIS WEEK**
Email the draft menus to your most engaged members. Ask: "Would you buy this? What's missing?" One conversation often reveals pricing friction or service gaps your head won't catch alone.

**5. Post it everywhere (digital + physical)**
Website homepage, printed at reception, coach WhatsApp group, member onboarding email. Repetition

kpis.Action: EXTERNAL: Refine our funnel landing pages to make it very clear what we offer, look at common questions
kpis.advice:
# Landing Page Execution for The Forge

• **Audit your actual drop-off points first** — Don't redesign blind. Pull your analytics: where are visitors leaving? (Homepage → pricing? Pricing → signup?) Talk to 5-10 women who visited but didn't join — ask "what confused you?" Your landing pages are failing at a specific moment. Find it before you write a word.

• **Structure around the objection stack women have about "women-only"** — This is your unique selling point *and* your biggest question mark. Lead with: "Why women-only matters" (safety, community, no male gaze), then immediately show proof (testimonials, before/afters from actual members). Most gyms bury this; make it your headline.

• **Create a "Questions We Hear" FAQ section above the fold** — Don't guess. Mine your email inquiries, Instagram DMs, and phone calls from the last 3 months. Exact phrases matter. If 8 women asked "do you have childcare?", that's your FAQ #1. Shows you understand her real concerns, not theoretical ones.

• **A/B test two versions of your main CTA** — One page says "Start Your 7-Day Free Trial" (low friction), another says "Book Your No-Sweat Intro" (commitment signal). Run each to 100 visitors. Your type of member might convert better on one. Most gym owners never test this.

• **Make pricing/offering crystal clear in one visual** — Use a simple comparison table or three boxes showing: what's included, what's *not*, and why. Women make buying decisions when uncertainty drops. One sentence per feature beats paragraphs.

kpis.Action: Implement internal member feedback system (simple monthly form or post-class check-in)
kpis.advice:
# Internal Feedback System - Execution Blueprint for Grace

• **Start with post-class verbal capture, not forms yet.** Have 2-3 staff grab 30 seconds with 5 random members after popular classes each week. Ask ONE question: "What's one thing we could do better?" Jot answers on a shared Google Sheet. This costs nothing, gives you real language, and builds the habit before designing a formal system.

• **Make the monthly form a 2-minute sprint, not a survey.** One digital form (Google Forms, embedded on your app/website) with 3 questions max: (1) How likely to recommend on 1-10? (2) One thing we nailed this month? (3) One thing to improve? Post it on class booking screens with a QR code. Incentivize: "Every month someone wins a free PT session."

• **Close the loop publicly and fast.** Share results in your member WhatsApp/group chat within 7 days with a "What we're fixing" action point. This proves feedback moves the needle and makes members feel heard—driving repeat responses. This alone increases participation 40%+ month-on-month.

• **Tie feedback directly to payroll conversations.** Use monthly trends in your team 1-on-1s. "Members said class pacing is off—Sarah, let's workshop this." Makes staff accountable and shows you're using data to improve *their* performance, not just collecting complaints.

**Start this week with post-class chats. Add the form in week 2. You'll have 30+ data points by month-end.**

kpis.Action: Write SOP's for membership members, personal training members, fuel forward, well75, strongevity. Every service should have a clear SOP.
kpis.advice:
# SOP Execution Plan for The Forge

**1. Reverse-engineer from your best clients first**
Don't start blank. Pick your 3 strongest membership/PT clients and document *exactly* what happens from day one—onboarding, communication cadence, what they actually do. This becomes your baseline SOP. Takes 4-6 hours but gives you real-world accuracy, not theoretical nonsense.

**2. Create one master SOP template, then fill per service**
Structure: Client Journey Stage → Specific Action → Who Does It → Timeline → Success Metric. Apply this template to all 5 services so they're consistent (easier to train staff) but customized (PT intensity ≠ Fuel Forward simplicity). One template = 2x faster to complete.

**3. Make Fuel Forward & Well75 your quick wins**
These are simpler, lower-touch services—write these SOPs first (2-3 days max). Win momentum + confidence before tackling the complex ones (memberships + Strongevity with their ongoing touchpoints).

**4. Embed the "retention trigger" into each SOP**
Every SOP needs 1-2 specific moments where staff re-engage/upsell (day 7 check-in, post-session feedback request, renewal nudge at month 11). SOPs that only describe operations miss revenue—yours shouldn't.

**5. Test-run each SOP with one real client, then adjust**
Paper SOPs fail. Have one staff member execute each SOP with a real new customer. Let them flag gaps/friction. Refine. Then publish. 1-week turnaround per service.

**Timeline: 6-8

kpis.Action: Establish a consistent social media content calendar with 3–4 posts/week planned in advance
kpis.advice:
# Social Media Calendar Execution for The Forge

**Don't build a fancy template first — start with what works NOW:**
- Audit your last 30 days of posts. Which 3-4 got the most engagement? Copy that format weekly (e.g., "Transformation Tuesday" before/afters, "Coach Q&A" reels, member spotlights). You're not guessing — you're doubling down on what already converts.

**Batch-create content in one 90-minute session, twice monthly:**
- Film 6-8 short clips on one day: member testimonials, 30-second workout demos, behind-the-scenes gym moments. Use your phone. Audio doesn't need to be perfect. Schedule them into a Google Sheet with captions pre-written. This kills the "I don't know what to post" paralysis.

**Assign ONE person (ideally you, 30 mins/week) to scheduling, not creating:**
- Use Buffer or Later to schedule posts at peak times (typically 6-7am and 5-6pm for Belfast fitness demographics). Set a recurring calendar reminder Tuesday morning to queue the week's content. This removes daily decision-making friction.

**Create a 3-post formula that rotates:**
- **Week 1**: Member win (testimonial/result) | Coach insight (quick tip) | Class highlight (new session announcement)
- **Week 2**: Same rotation, different member/tip/class
- Repeat. Consistency beats novelty — your audience doesn't need surprises, they need predictability and proof you're active.

**Track ONE metric only:** New DM inquiries per week. If posts aren't driving inquiries, the problem isn

kpis.Action: Schedule regular videography sessions every 8 weeks with Jordan
kpis.advice:
# Executing the Videography Sessions with Jordan

**Lock in the calendar NOW — don't wing it**
Block the next 6 months on your calendar immediately (every 8 weeks). Send Jordan a calendar invite TODAY with confirmed dates. This prevents the "we'll schedule it later" death spiral. Treat it like a client session — non-negotiable.

**Pre-session brief = better content, less wasted time**
3 days before each shoot, send Jordan a one-page brief with: (1) specific transformation stories you want captured that month, (2) 2-3 key messaging angles (new class launch, member wins, facility highlight), (3) locations/members to feature. This means Jordan shoots strategically, not generically.

**Batch your content usage immediately after shoot**
Don't wait. Within 48 hours of the session, identify: what goes on Instagram Stories (raw, weekly), what's a Reel (edited, high-energy), what's testimonial content. Assign posting dates. This prevents footage sitting unused and keeps momentum visible to your audience.

**Make it revenue-positive from day one**
Plan what content drives actions: testimonial clips should link to trial signups, transformation content should go in email nurture sequences to warm leads. Track which videos convert best — then brief Jordan on repeating those angles.

**Tie videography to a specific growth target**
Each 8-week cycle should support ONE goal: new class launch, member retention campaign, or lead generation push. This keeps the spend connected to actual business results, not "content for content's sake."

kpis.Action: Revisit systemize automations for 6 week trial enquiries
kpis.advice:
# Systemize Automations for 6-Week Trial Enquiries — Concrete Actions

• **Audit the current leakage first** — Map exactly where trial enquiries drop off (WhatsApp? Email? Phone?). Identify which prospects never convert vs. those who do. You'll likely find 40-60% never get a follow-up within 24 hours. Fix *that* first before automating the wrong process.

• **Build a 3-touch auto-sequence in your CRM (or Zapier + email tool if you're not using CRM yet)** — Day 1: Automated welcome + trial booking link (deadline: 14 days), Day 3: "Spots filling up" nudge with member testimonial video, Day 7: Final reminder + offer (e.g., first session + nutrition chat free). Each touch takes 90 seconds to write; do it today.

• **Automate the *pre-trial* friction, not just the pitch** — Send automated check-in 24hrs before their trial (time/location/what to bring). This cuts no-shows by 25-35%. Use your gym's WhatsApp Business or SMS tool (Twilio, SimpleTexting) — response rates beat email by 3x.

• **Tag & segment as they move through** — When someone books a trial, auto-tag them "Trial_Booked." After they show up, tag "Trial_Completed." This lets you send the right message to the right person (converting trialists get different messaging than ghosts). Takes 10 minutes to set up segments.

• **Kill the manual reminder game** — Remove yourself from sending "Did you book yet?" messages. Replace with

kpis.Action: Finalise and document weekly action checklist for each role (JoJo, Eilis, Sarah) with KPIs they own
kpis.advice:
# Weekly Role Checklists + KPIs for The Forge

**For JoJo (assume: Operations/Floor Manager):**
- **Action items**: Class schedule execution, equipment maintenance log, member check-ins on 3 lapsed accounts/week
- **KPI she owns**: Class attendance % (target: 75%+), equipment downtime hours (target: <2/week), lapsed member reactivations (target: 1/week)
- **Document as**: Single-page checklist (Mon-Fri tasks) + attendance/downtime tracker she updates daily—not a separate spreadsheet she'll ignore

**For Eilis (assume: Sales/Community):**
- **Action items**: 5 new member consultations booked, 2 referral follow-ups, 1 local partnership outreach
- **KPI she owns**: New member sign-ups (target: X/week), referral conversion rate (%), partnership pipeline value
- **Document as**: CRM tasks with due dates + weekly win/loss log so you both see what's sticking

**For Sarah (assume: Content/Social):**
- **Action items**: 4 Instagram posts minimum, 1 member spotlight feature, 1 email to inactive members
- **KPI she owns**: Engagement rate (likes + comments per post), email open/click rate, social-to-inquiry conversion
- **Document as**: Content calendar linked to engagement tracker—she can't own KPIs she can't see weekly

**The execution move**: Print these, laminate them, review together every Friday 15 mins. Each person rates themselves 1-5 on KPI hit. Celebrate wins. Troubleshoot blockers

kpis.Action: Run team vision session — share the SSO, 90-day plan, and each person's lane clearly
kpis.advice:
# Vision Session Execution for The Forge

**Before the session (48hrs prior):**
- Send each team member a 1-pager with YOUR personal "why" for The Forge + 3 specific wins you want in 90 days (e.g., "50 new members," "zero late cancellations," "5-star Google rating"). Let them digest it—don't surprise them cold.

**The session itself (90 mins max):**
- Start with 10 mins of you talking about the SSO (Single Shared Objective)—make it ONE number they can visualize. "We're hitting 200 active members by June." Not multiple priorities.
- Spend 40 mins on the 90-day plan: map it as a visual timeline on the wall/screen. Show the 3 phases. Point to *exactly* which weeks require what from whom.
- Assign "lanes" by capability, not title. Not "you manage this project"—but "You own member onboarding experience start-to-finish. Success looks like: X. You decide how." Give them the outcome, not the method.

**The close (critical):**
- Each person states back *their* lane in one sentence. If they get it wrong, you clarify immediately. Don't let ambiguity walk out the door.
- Book 1-on-1 check-ins for week 1 (10 mins each). That's where real buy-in happens.

**Why this works for you:** Your team needs to see the gym's heartbeat, not a slide deck. They need autonomy within clear guardrails.

kpis.Action: Audit workload distribution — identify who is over-stretched and rebalance over first 30 days
kpis.advice:
# Workload Audit & Rebalancing — 30-Day Action Plan

• **Map actual hours NOW, not perceived load.** Have each team member (coaches, front desk, management) log what they're doing for 3 days this week—down to 15-min blocks. You'll spot where time actually goes vs. what you think. This beats guessing and reveals hidden tasks stealing capacity.

• **Identify the 20% of tasks eating 80% of someone's time.** Once you have the data, pull out the repetitive/low-value work (admin, scheduling, cleaning tasks) that's choking your star coaches. These are your rebalance opportunities—can they be automated, delegated to part-time staff, or batched differently?

• **Create a "swaps board" with your team in week 2.** Show them the raw data. Ask directly: *"What would free you up most? What could someone else handle?"* You'll find quick wins (maybe someone hates social media but excels at programming—swap it). This builds buy-in because *they* help design the fix.

• **Hire or reallocate ONE role first—the bottleneck position.** Don't try to rebalance everything. Identify who's at 110% and either split their role, bring in part-time support for that function, or shuffle responsibilities. Fix the critical break-point first; knock-on effects handle the rest.

• **Lock in the new structure by end of week 3.** Publish the new schedule/responsibilities and measure again in week 4. You're looking for visible relief—people working ~45-50 hours max, with measurable headspace.

What's your biggest hunch

# Cost Reduction Playbook for The Forge

**Before you cut anything:**
- Audit your actual spend for 60 days (utilities, staffing, inventory, suppliers). You'll find 10% fat without touching quality. Most gyms overpay on cleaning contracts and energy—low-hanging fruit.

**Hit your biggest cost drivers first:**
- **Staffing** (likely 40-50% of costs): Shift to part-time PTs instead of salaried; tie bonuses to membership retention, not hours. This cuts payroll without reducing service.
- **Utilities**: Get 3 competitive quotes for electricity/heating. Women's gyms run hot—negotiate a rate lock for 12 months now.

**Supplier negotiations (do this THIS WEEK):**
- Call your equipment maintenance, cleaning, and laundry suppliers. Tell them you're shopping competitors. You'll get 10-15% discounts immediately—they'd rather keep you than lose you.

**Revenue protection angle:**
- Don't cut what members see (cleanliness, class quality, equipment). Cut internal waste only. A cheaper gym still feels cheap; a well-run gym at fair price builds loyalty.

**Quick math check:**
- If your monthly costs are ~£X, you need to find ~£X/10. Map it: if staffing is £8k/month, a 5% shift there is £400. Suppliers at £2k gives you £200. Utilities at £1.5k gives you £150. You're done with zero member impact.

**What's your biggest monthly cost category?** That's where the 10% actually lives.

# How Grace Should Execute: Monthly Finance KPI Updates

• **Set a non-negotiable 3-hour block on the same day each month** (e.g., first Friday) — don't let it drift. Pull membership revenue, churn rate, cost-per-acquisition, and gross margin LIVE from your POS/payment system. If you're manually spreadsheet-ing, you're wasting time you could spend on growth.

• **Track the 4 metrics that actually drive decisions**: monthly recurring revenue (MRR), member churn %, payroll as % of revenue, and cash runway. Ignore vanity numbers. Flag any metric moving >5% month-on-month as a trigger for immediate investigation (not next month).

• **Create a 1-page visual dashboard you glance at daily** — not a 20-sheet workbook. Graph churn and MRR trends so you spot problems before they compound. Share it with whoever handles ops/finances so they own the numbers too.

• **Use one metric to drive weekly action**: if churn is creeping up, that week's priority is retention conversations. If acquisition cost is rising, audit where new members come from. The KPI update is only useful if it changes what you do.

• **Automate the repetitive math** — use a template or simple accounting software so you're analyzing, not data-entry. Those 3 hours should be thinking about growth levers, not clicking cells.

# Financial Forecasting Execution for The Forge

• **Pull 12 months of actual membership data THIS WEEK** — transaction dates, churn dates, payment amounts, class attendance by member. Don't estimate. This is your only reliable input. If data's scattered, hire a virtual bookkeeper for 4 hours to consolidate it into one clean spreadsheet.

• **Model three scenarios, not one:** Base case (current churn + growth trend), pessimistic (20% churn spike if competitor opens), optimistic (add 15 new members/month if you nail referral program). Run each through 24 months. This beats "best guess" every time and shows where you're most vulnerable.

• **Focus the forecast on YOUR actual levers** — membership tiers (do you know LTV by tier?), class capacity utilization %, seasonal dips (January surge, summer drop?), and variable costs you can actually control (staff hours, supplies). Skip industry benchmarks; your numbers matter.

• **Output a single dashboard:** Monthly recurring revenue, cash runway, breakeven point on any new investment, and one red-flag metric you'll check weekly (e.g., churn rate). Share it with your accountant for 30 mins of QA — catches errors that derail decisions.

• **Use this to kill two birds:** You now have the data to pitch investors/banks AND to make real decisions on hiring, expansion, or marketing spend next month. Don't forecast then file it.

What's your current churn rate, roughly?

kpis.Action: Map profitability of each service (classes vs PT vs add-ons) — build a simple per-service P&L
kpis.advice:
# Profitability Mapping for The Forge

**Get your numbers organized RIGHT NOW:**
- Pull 3 months of revenue from your payment system (Stripe, Square, whatever you use) and sort by service type. Don't estimate—export actual data. Takes 30 mins max.
- Add up your *fixed* costs monthly (rent, insurance, staff salaries, equipment leases) and allocate roughly proportional to each service (e.g., if PT uses one studio 40% of the time, assign 40% of rent to PT).

**Build the actual P&L (one spreadsheet, one hour):**
- Columns: Classes | PT | Add-ons (merch, supplements, challenges, etc.)
- Rows: Total revenue | Direct variable costs (instructor pay per class, any PT commissions) | Allocated fixed costs | Gross profit | Margin %
- This instantly shows you which service subsidizes which (spoiler: PT usually dominates, classes are thin-margin volume plays).

**Act on what it reveals:**
- If classes are 15% margin but PT is 45%, you know to shift your marketing spend and staff incentives toward PT upsells in class.
- If add-ons are currently 2% of revenue but could be 8%, that's your easiest profit lever—focus there before hiring more instructors.
- Share the numbers with your team so they stop guessing and start selling the profitable stuff.

Done right, this takes 2 hours and changes how you make every decision for the next year.

kpis.Action: Audit all active systemize automations — remove duplicates, confirm enquiry-to-trial flow works end to end.
kpis.advice:
# Audit & Fix Your Automations — Tactical Execution

• **Map the actual flow first (30 mins):** Pull a real enquiry from the last week. Screenshot/document exactly what happens: email received → which automation triggers → what the prospect sees → when trial gets booked. You'll spot duplicates and breaks immediately. Don't assume it works.

• **Kill the duplicates ruthlessly:** Go into your automation tool (Zapier/Make/native software) and list every active automation by trigger. If two automations fire on "new enquiry," one of them is probably sending duplicate welcome emails or double-booking trials. Delete the older/weaker version today—don't "review later."

• **Test the weak link:** The enquiry-to-trial flow breaks 90% of the time at the trial-booking step. Verify: Does your booking link actually populate in the email? Does it auto-sync to your calendar AND CRM? Send yourself a test enquiry right now and book a trial—if you hit friction, your prospects are too.

• **Set a 48-hour response rule in automation:** Enquiry comes in → automated response goes out within 2 hours with trial link + your direct number. Any manual follow-up after that is a backup, not the system. This kills prospects going cold.

• **Audit schedule:** Do this quarterly. Set a calendar reminder for next month to run one real test enquiry through the whole system—takes 15 mins, catches breakage before it costs you members.

What tool are you currently using for automations?

kpis.Action: Pick 3 repetitive tasks and build an AI automation
kpis.advice:
# AI Automation for The Forge — Concrete Action Plan

**1. Start with Member Onboarding Emails (Highest ROI)**
- Build a simple Zapier workflow: New member signup → ChatGPT prompt → Auto-send personalized welcome sequence (day 1: welcome + gym tour video link, day 3: nutrition basics, day 7: progress check-in)
- This replaces 30+ mins of manual emailing weekly. Use your existing email platform (Mailchimp/ActiveCampaign) to trigger it
- **Action today:** Export last 10 member signups, map the exact emails you send—this is your blueprint

**2. Automate Class Booking Reminders & No-Show Reduction**
- Use your gym management software (Mindbody/Mariana Tek) + Make.com to auto-send SMS 24hrs before bookings: "Class tomorrow at 6pm. Reply CONFIRM or lose spot"
- Track which members have highest no-show rates—the system flags them automatically
- **Action today:** Check your current no-show % and calculate $ lost per month (this funds your automation ROI case)

**3. Social Media Post Scheduling + Caption Generation**
- Batch film 3 workout clips weekly, upload to Google Drive. Use Zapier + GPT-4 to auto-generate 3 caption variations (motivational, educational, community angle) for Instagram/TikTok
- Schedule via Buffer or Later—saves ~5 hours weekly
- **Action today:** Identify your top 5 performing posts. Write the exact tone/keywords ChatGPT should use

**Choose #1 first.** It

kpis.Action: Start Cyber security for forge website
kpis.advice:
# Cyber Security for The Forge Website — Execution Plan

• **Get an SSL certificate TODAY (costs £0-50/year).** This encrypts customer data and shows a padlock in browsers. Use Let's Encrypt (free) or your hosting provider's one-click install. Non-negotiable before taking ANY payments online.

• **Audit what customer data you're actually storing.** Do you collect card details, or does Stripe handle it? Email addresses? Phone numbers? You only secure what you've identified. Most gyms over-collect unnecessarily—delete what you don't need.

• **Implement basic access controls NOW.** Change all default passwords on your website admin panel, hosting, and any linked accounts (email, payment processors). Use a password manager. Add two-factor authentication (2FA) to your WordPress/hosting login if available.

• **Plug the revenue-killer leak:** Set up automated backups (your hosting usually offers this for £2-5/month). One ransomware attack that deletes member records = business shutdown. Test one backup restore to confirm it actually works.

• **Get a liability statement live.** Add a basic privacy policy + terms of service to your website footer. Use a template generator (Termly, Iubenda—free tier works). This isn't just cyber hygiene; it's legal protection and builds member trust.

**Priority order:** SSL → password audit → backups → 2FA → legal docs. Week 1 focus: SSL + password reset.

marketing.advice:
**Insights**

Your CPL has exploded 350% since April (£6→£27) whilst spend has collapsed 75% (£435→£109). This isn't a budget problem—it's a creative and audience exhaustion problem. You've systematically burned through your best-performing segments and your creative is now stale. The 75% close rate in July is still strong, so the leads *are* qualified; you're just reaching fewer of the right people. July's spend cut was reactive when the real issue started in May (CPL doubled month-on-month).

Your April cohort (69 leads at £6) outperforms July 5.5x on efficiency *with similar close rates implied*—that audience and creative combination still exists but you've stopped reaching it. June's decline to 21 leads suggests the algorithm knew your best audiences were saturated by then; July's cliff is the result.

**Actions**

1. **Reactivate April's top-performing segments immediately.** Pull your April ad account data: which age bands, interests, and lookalike audiences delivered that £6 CPL? Build fresh creative (video/carousel, not static) around those exact cohorts and test with £150/week budget. You're not finding new people; you're re-engaging the ones who actually convert.

2. **Test three completely new creative angles this month.** Your July creative is dead weight. Run parallel tests: (a) testimonial-led video from recent clients emphasising the women-only community angle, (b) pain-point carousel (tired of mixed gyms / intimidation / lack of progress) leading with solution, (c) limited-time urgency (trial spots filling fast). Allocate £20 to each as learning campaigns; kill the worst after 5 days, double the best.

3. **Widen your audience targeting but tighten your retargeting.** Stop relying solely on cold audiences—July's tiny lead volume suggests you're talking to people outside your conversion window. Layer in a 90-day website visitor retargeting audience (even if small) and test 35–55 age range separately from 25–35. Your April data likely skews older; confirm this and bias budget there.

4. **Set a non-
